perturbation.original_text: Distributor shall pay Company within thirty (30) days of Distributor's or, as the case may be, the end-user's receipt of Products.
perturbation.changed_text: Distributor shall pay Company within sixty (60) days of Distributor's or, as the case may be, the end-user's receipt of Products.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the payment terms to 60 days contradicts the Illinois Uniform Commercial Code, which implies a shorter reasonable time if not specified and industry standards usually dictate net 30.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Illinois Uniform Commercial Code - Implied Terms for Payment
perturbation.law_explanation: While not explicitly stating a time limit, the UCC implies payment within a reasonable time if not otherwise agreed. Changing the payment terms to 60 days, especially considering standard practices (e.g., net 30), could be deemed unreasonable, potentially conflicting with UCC expectations for prompt payment.

perturbation.justification: The modification extends the payment timeframe from 30 to 60 days. While the provided law snippets are limited, the Law Explanation and Perturbation Explanation suggest this extension may conflict with the UCC's implied 'reasonable time' for payment and industry standards (e.g., net 30). The change moves the payment deadline from a potentially reasonable timeframe to one that might be considered unreasonable, creating a contradiction based on the information provided.

perturbation.original_text: Following expiration or termination of this Agreement, the Distributor may continue to sell any Products in the Market which are in its inventory Page -9- and which the Company has not repurchased.
perturbation.changed_text: Following expiration or termination of this Agreement, the Distributor may continue to sell any Products in the Market which are in its inventory Page -9- and which the Company has not repurchased for a period not to exceed 180 days.
perturbation.explanation: Limiting the post-termination sales period to 180 days may conflict with Illinois law regarding the winding up of business affairs after termination, particularly if it unduly restricts the distributor's ability to liquidate inventory fairly.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Illinois Business Corporation Act - Winding Up
perturbation.law_explanation: The Illinois Business Corporation Act allows for a reasonable period for winding up affairs after dissolution, which includes selling off existing inventory. Arbitrarily limiting this to 180 days could be seen as an unreasonable restriction, especially if a longer period is needed to fairly liquidate the inventory, thereby conflicting with the implied right to wind up business affairs.

If the source note at the end of a Section of the statutes includes a Public Act that has not yet taken effect, the version of the law that is currently in effect may have already been removed from the database and you should refer to that Public Act to see the changes made to the current law. 805 ILCS 5/12.15 (805 ILCS 5/12.15) (from Ch. 32, par. 12.15) Sec. 12.15. Voluntary dissolution by vote of shareholders. Dissolution of a corporation may be authorized by a vote of shareholders, in the following manner: (a) Either: (1) The board of directors shall adopt a resolution, which may be with or without their recommendation, proposing that the corporation be dissolved voluntarily, and directing that the question of such dissolution be submitted to a vote at a meeting of shareholders, which may be either an annual or special meeting, or (2) Holders of not less than one-fifth of the votes of the shares entitled to vote on dissolution may, in writing, propose the dissolution of the corporation to the board of directors; if the directors fail or refuse to call a meeting of shareholders to consider such proposal for more than one year after delivery thereof, the shareholders proposing dissolution may call a meeting of the shareholders to consider such proposal. (b) Written notice stating that the purpose, or one of the purposes, of the shareholders' meeting is to consider the voluntary dissolution of the corporation, shall be given to each shareholder whether or not entitled to vote at such meeting within the time and in the manner provided in this Act for the giving of notice of meetings of shareholders. If such meeting be an annual meeting, such purpose may be included in the notice of such annual meeting. (c) At such meeting a vote of the shareholders entitled to vote on dissolution shall be taken on the resolution to dissolve voluntarily the corporation, which shall require for its adoption the affirmative votes of at least two-thirds of the votes of the shares entitled to vote on dissolution, unless any class of shares is entitled to vote as a class in respect thereof, in which event the resolution shall require for its adoption the affirmative votes of at least two-thirds of the votes of the shares of each class of shares entitled to vote as a class in respect thereof and
perturbation.justification: The changed text limits the distributor's ability to sell products post-termination to 180 days. The Law Explanation states that the Illinois Business Corporation Act allows for a 'reasonable period' for winding up affairs, including selling inventory. By setting a definitive 180-day limit, the changed text contradicts the 'reasonable period' standard, potentially hindering fair liquidation, as described in the Perturbation and Law Explanations. The Scraped Law Snippet 1 discusses voluntary dissolution by shareholders, implying a process that includes winding up business affairs, which the 180-day limit may impede.

perturbation.original_text: Company will carry a reasonable amount of product liability insurance through a reasonably acceptable products liability insurance company and will name the Distributor as an additional insured under that policy. Company will make reasonable efforts to procure a policy, which is non-cancelable, except upon thirty (30) days, advance notice to the Distributor.
perturbation.changed_text: Company will carry a reasonable amount of product liability insurance through a reasonably acceptable products liability insurance company and will name the Distributor as an additional insured under that policy. Company will make reasonable efforts to procure a policy, which is non-cancelable, except upon ten (10) days, advance notice to the Distributor.
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the notice period for cancellation of product liability insurance to 10 days may violate general principles of commercial reasonableness and good faith, potentially leaving the distributor inadequately protected and unable to secure alternative coverage promptly.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Implied Covenant of Good Faith and Fair Dealing (Illinois)
perturbation.law_citation: See Dayan v. McDonald's Corp., 125 Ill. App. 3d 972, 466 N.E.2d 958 (1st Dist. 1984)
perturbation.law_explanation: Illinois law implies a covenant of good faith and fair dealing in every contract. While there's no explicit statute dictating the minimum notice for insurance cancellation in this context, reducing the notice to 10 days may be deemed commercially unreasonable and a breach of good faith, as it could unfairly prejudice the distributor's ability to maintain adequate liability coverage. The 30 day notice in the original contract language is already relatively short and reducing that further harms the distributor.

perturbation.justification: The modification reduces the notice period for insurance cancellation from 30 to 10 days. The Law Explanation suggests this could breach the implied covenant of good faith under Illinois law, as it unfairly prejudices the distributor's ability to maintain adequate liability coverage. The original 30-day notice was already short, and shortening it further exacerbates the harm. Although the scraped snippets are unhelpful, the Law and Perturbation Explanations focus on the principal that reduced notice harms the distributor by limiting time to obtain new insurance. This represents a serious erosion of the distributor's protection and creates a contradiction.
